DAY 4
Language is
the Interface
Motion · Images · Slides

“
The direction was the design.
— Alfred Hitchcock 70 shots for 45 seconds — all pre-planned.
Why pre-production matters
Improvisation increases costs.
Precision and planning reduce them.
Our tools will improvise unless you constrain them. Your job today: give them a precise “story board”.
What you'll leave with
Remotion
Prompt Claude to generate a short, on-brand video
Gemini
Extract a visual style and turn it into a reusable Gem for consistent image output
Power-Design
Generate a branded slide deck
Last time: Figma Make  ·  Skills / MCP / CLI  ·  Design is code
Today's agenda
1 Create videos with Claude + Remotion 0:15–0:45
2 Consistent image styles with Gemini (Nano Banana + Gems) 0:45–1:15
3 Create slides with Claude (power-design) 1:15–1:45
4 Bonus: Google Stitch if time
5 Q&A + homework 1:45–2:00
Section 01
Create Videos with
Claude + Remotion
Value · How it works · Demo · Hands-on
Your shot list for Remotion.
Motion is design's slowest loop.
Why it matters
Motion is the highest-effort, lowest-iteration part of design — Remotion + Claude collapses that loop
Explainer videos, animated charts, social cuts, onboarding sequences — no timeline editor required
It's code: versionable, reviewable, reusable — the opposite of a one-off After Effects file
What you can build
Animated explainers & kinetic typography
Data-driven charts and dashboards rendered to video
Programmatic social content (one template, 100 variants)
Voiceover sync (TTS or imported audio)
What you'll walk away knowing
A mental model of Remotion as "React for video"
How to set up the Remotion skill in Claude Code
How to prompt for animations, voiceovers, and data-driven scenes
The pipeline
Create a folder
→
Add Remotion skill
→
npx remotion studio
→
Prompt Claude
→
Preview live
→
Export MP4
React: reusable components that re-render when data changes.
Demo
5 min
1 Open a folder with the Remotion skill already installed
2 Prompt Claude: "Create a 15-second explainer video with 3 scenes. Add a voiceover."
3 Show live preview in the Remotion studio
4 Iterate once — change pacing, swap a color token, regenerate
5 Export the final video
Honest limitations
Not the right tool for totally unique, one-off cinematic animations
Render times are slow for complex scenes
Preview is fast. Export is not.
Debugging is code-shaped
Read errors, don't nudge keyframes.
Your turn · 15 min
Your turn
1Add the Remotion skill from the Skool link
2Create a folder and open it in Claude Code
3Get the Remotion skills:
npx skills add https://github.com/remotion-dev/skills --skill remotion-best-practices
4Run /remotion-starter
5Prompt your video — pick one:
3 animated slides on "how to learn a handstand"
An animated pie chart from data you provide
Something from the Remotion gallery (avoid heavy scenes)
6Add a voiceover — Apple TTS or ElevenLabs
7Post your render or screen recording in chat
✅ Done when: You've posted a 10–15s rendered video or preview recording in chat.
If stuck: [backup link placeholder]
Create product demo
1Find a landing page of a product you want to create a demo for
2Copy the URL
3Paste the URL with a prompt:
"Please create a brief product demo for the specific website. When you created, try using the same fonts, the same logo, and the same picture that you find within the page. So feel free to take screenshot and adapt as you prefer."
4Take any brand from the power-design skill, drag the folder into the chat and prompt:
"Theme the entire video in the [your brand] theme — do not use hard coded values, only use the themes found in the design system."
5Add voiceover and music with the OpenRouter API. Prompt:
"Create a script, create voice over and add music to the video." (it will ask for your OpenRouter API key)
You just made motion repeatable.
Next: make your images repeatable.
Section 02
Consistent Image Styles
with Gemini
Nano Banana · Gems · Team workflows
Your shot list for Gemini.
The consistency problem.
Why it matters
The hardest part isn't generating one image — it's generating the same style across 50 images, 5 designers, 6 months
Nano Banana + Gems let you capture a style once and replay it consistently
What you'll walk away knowing
How to prompt Nano Banana for high-quality results
How to extract a visual style from a reference and encode it as a Gem
How to share that Gem with your team as a "style template"
How it works
Subject
·
Style
·
Composition
·
Lighting
·
Medium
Describe each separately.
Nano Banana
Google's image model. High-fidelity, editable. Use for marketing illustrations, hero imagery, spot illustrations.
Gems
Reusable Gemini configurations — saved system prompts. Shareable with your team so everyone generates in the same style.
Iterate with edit prompts, not full re-rolls. Use reference images to lock in style.
Demo
5 min
1 Show a reference illustration (foodora example)
2 Prompt Nano Banana to extract the style description
3 Create a Gem using that description
4 Generate 3 new illustrations in that style — show consistency
5 Repeat for a photo-realistic style (e.g. product shots)
Honest limitations
Complex compositions with many subjects still drift
Gems are tied to the Google ecosystem
Not portable to other LLMs.
Photo-realistic faces and hands still need careful prompting and review
Your turn · 15 min
Your turn
1Pick a reference image (your brand, Pinterest, or Midjourney)
2Upload to Gemini → prompt:
"Describe the visual style of this image in enough detail to recreate it."
3Open Gems editor → paste style as system prompt → add guardrails
"Always use this palette", "never add text in the image", etc.
4Make one Gem for illustrations + one for photo-realistic images
5Share your Gem link + one image from each in the chat
✅ Done when: You've posted one illustration + one photo-realistic image generated from your Gems in chat.
If stuck: [backup link placeholder]
You just made images repeatable.
Next: make decks repeatable.
Section 03
Create Slides
with Claude
power-design · 20 design rules
Your shot list for Power-Design.
Why slides still look like AI.
The problem
"AI slop" slides all look the same — the model has no design system to anchor on
The fix: design is code. Codify what "good" looks like (20 rules + brand spec) → Claude generates decks that don't look AI-generated
The skill defines what makes a good slide and applies it every time
What you'll walk away knowing
How to generate and iterate on a branded slide deck with Claude
How to add animations from 21st.dev
The pipeline
1 Download the power-design repo
2 Open it in Claude Code
3 Tell it which brand to use
4 Prompt → get a full HTML deck
5 Preview in browser, iterate by prompting
Demo
5 min
1 Show the power-design repo open in Claude Code
2 Prompt Claude to generate a 3-slide deck using extracted brand + 20 rules
3 Open the preview in the browser
4 Iterate: "increase whitespace on slide 2", "use less contrast on the background"
5 Copy-paste an animation from 21st.dev into a slide
Honest limitations
Setup is heavier than "open Keynote"
You need the skill + a design.md file.
Output is HTML/CSS — not .pptx
Great for the web; not for clients who insist on PowerPoint.
Not great for real-time collaboration
Unless you collab on git together.
Iteration is prompt-based, not direct manipulation
Small visual tweaks can take several prompts.
Your turn · 15 min
Your turn
1Download power-design skill + awesome-design-md
2Open in Claude → run /awesome-design + /power-design
3Prompt: "Generate a 5-slide deck introducing X, using the brand stripe" → open preview
4Make 2 directed improvements via prompts
whitespace, hierarchy, copy, or layout
5Find an animation on 21st.dev → copy the prompt → tell Claude which slide to add it to
✅ Done when: You've posted your live preview or published URL in chat.
If stuck: [backup link placeholder]
You just made decks repeatable.
Now let's talk about what's coming next.
Top 3 takeaways
1
Language is the interface — if you can describe it (video, image, slide), you can build it
2
Consistency comes from codifying taste: skills, gems, brand specs, and rulebooks
3
The best workflow is rarely one tool — it's Claude orchestrating Remotion, Gemini, and your design system together
Questions?
10 min
Prompt to Product · Day 4
Bonus
Google Stitch
If time — ~20 min
When to reach for Stitch instead of Figma.
Stitch vs Figma
Stitch: start from a text prompt; AI builds the design system and the screens
Figma: design system already exists; AI is an add-on, not the canvas
Notable features
Auto-generated design system (tokens, type, base components)
Variations panel — vary layout, color, imagery, typography, or copy
Annotate — drag to highlight a region and describe a change
Predictive heatmap — one-click attention/focus estimate
Export to Figma (auto-layout preserved, mostly)
When to reach for Stitch: exploration, ideation, accelerators. Not yet a daily driver.
Early release · component library limited · desktop↔mobile drift
Demo + Your turn
Demo — 5 steps
aOpen Stitch, pick "Web." Prompt: "Build me a crypto dashboard in dark mode with purple as primary."
bTweak the design system live (color, font, radius)
cGenerate 2–3 additional pages; run Variations on one
dAnnotate a region; run predictive heatmap
eExport one screen to Figma, inspect layers
Your turn — 5 steps
aOpen Stitch, pick web or mobile
bPrompt: "Build me a [product] dashboard in [light/dark] mode with [brand color] as primary."
cTweak one token (color, radius, or font)
dAdd 2–3 pages; run Variations on your main screen
eExport to Figma → paste into blank file → make one edit
✅ Done when: you've exported one screen to Figma.
